- Abstract 16
- Plant resistance ( R )  and pathogen avirulence ( Avr )  gene interactions are central to 17 pathogen recognition and disease resistance in crops. However, functional 18 characterisation  of Avr effectors  in Puccinia  striiformis f.  sp. tritici ( Pst )  remains 19 challenging due to its obligate biotrophic lifestyle. Here, we used a high-throughput 20 wheat  protoplast-based  screen  to  evaluate Avr / R interactions  efficiently,  enabling 21 rapid detection of effector-induced defence responses across diverse wheat 22 cultivars. We identified an Avr candidate  that  we  termed AvrPstB48 as a defence23

activating effector,  consistently triggering  defence  responses  in  16  out  of  24 24 independent wheat cultivars tested. AvrPstB48 is hemizygous, and the Pst genome 25 carries four divergent paralogs within a single gene cluster on separate haplotypes. 26 Analysis  of  these  paralogs  revealed  partial  redundancy  in  their  abilities  to  activate 27 wheat defences and enabled us to identify a single amino acid in AvrPstB48, which 28 is  necessary  but  not  sufficient  for  defence  activation.  Notably,  the  activation  of 29 defence by AvrPstB48 in protoplasts did not consistently translate into resistance in 30 planta .  Whole-plant  infection  assays  revealed  that  some  cultivars  that  exhibited 31 strong defence activation in the protoplast assay remained fully susceptible to Pst . 32 However,  comparison  of  infection  dynamics  between  the AvrPstB48recognising 33 cultivar Avocet  S  and  the  non-recognising susceptible  cultivar Morocco  revealed  a 34 delay in disease progression in Avocet S, evidenced by reduced mycelial growth and 35 increased callose deposition at 10 days post-inoculation. This supports the 36 hypothesis  that  while AvrPstB48 is  recognised  by  specific  host  genotypes,  its 37 immune  activation  is  actively  suppressed in  planta by  additional Pst -encoded 38 suppressor effectors. 39

Keywords: Puccinia  striiformis f.  sp. tritici ,  effectors,  avirulence  genes,  protoplast screening, wheat resistance, plant immunity.

INTRODUCTION

Plant pathogenic fungi are major contributors to crop losses globally, with diseases 45 such as wheat stripe rust caused by Puccinia striiformis f. sp. tritic i  ( Pst ) continuing 46 to impact wheat production worldwide, driven by ongoing intercontinental spread and 47

genetic diversification  (Hovmøller et al., 2010;  2025). The infection  strategy  of this 48 fungus includes the  secretion  of  large  sets  of  so-called  effector  proteins  into  plant 49 tissue  (Wang  et  al.,  2023).  Such  effectors  modulate  host  cellular  processes  to 50 suppress  defence  responses  and  enhance  pathogen  colonisation  (Zheng  et  al., 51 2023). Identification of recognised effectors and their functional characterisation are 52 aspects towards advancing management strategies. 53 A subset of effectors designated avirulence ( Avr ) effectors are detected by resistance 54 ( R )  genes  in  certain  host  genotypes  (Xia  et  al.,  2020). Avr recognition  triggers  a 55 myriad of defence responses, including transcriptional reprogramming that restricts 56 pathogen colonisation (Dodds & Rathjen, 2010). Several Avr genes from wheat rust 57 fungi  have been cloned. For example, cloning of Avr genes from P. graminis f.  sp. 58 tritici ( Pgt ),  including AvrSr50 (Chen  et  al.,  2017), AvrSr35 (Salcedo  et  al.,  2017), 59 AvrSr27 (Upadhyaya et al., 2021; Outram et al., 2024), AvrSr62 (Chen et al. 2024), 60 AvrSr22 and AvrSr13 (Arndell  et  al.,  2024),  has  provided  detailed  insights  into 61 pathogen recognition. Similar advances have been reported for Blumeria graminis , 62 with  the  identification  of AvrPm2 (Praz  et  al.,  2017)  and AvrPm3 (Bourras  et  al., 63 2019), and Zymoseptoria tritici fungi, with the identification of AvrStb6 (Zhong et al., 64 2017), Avr3D1 (Meile et al., 2018) , AvrStb9 (Amezrou et al., 2023) and Zt-11 (Karki 65 et al., 2024). Additionally, recent studies in P. triticina have identified novel effectors 66 that may play key roles in virulence and host specificity, such as Pt1641 (Chang et 67 al., 2024), AvrLr21 (Shen et al., 2024) and AvrLr15 (Cui et al., 2024). 68 Many immune recognition  events  in  plants  are  mediated  by  intracellular  receptors 69 known  as  nucleotide-binding  leucine-rich  repeat  (NLR)  proteins,  which  are  often 70 encoded  by R genes.  Upon  recognising  specific  Avr effectors, NLRs  initiate 71 downstream defence responses such as transcriptional reprogramming and 72

programmed  cell  death.  Some  effectors  interfere  with  shared  immune  signalling 73 nodes that integrate pattern recognition receptor (PRR) and NLR-mediated 74 responses  or  modulate  host  metabolic  pathways  to  subvert  immunity  (Ramírez75 Zavaleta et al., 2022; Rhodes et al., 2022). Thus, effectors not only activate immune 76 responses but can suppress them, leading to effector-triggered susceptibility (ETS) 77 (Win  et  al.,  2012).  These  highlight  the  dual  roles  of  effectors  in  virulence  and 78 immunity and the complex interplay of recognition and suppression in plant-pathogen 79 interactions. 80

Despite significant progress in cloning wheat R genes, the identification of 81 corresponding Avr genes  in  rust  fungi  has  lagged,  largely  due  to  the  pathogen's 82 dikaryotic  genome  organisation  and  obligate  biotrophic  nature,  which  hinders in 83 planta functional studies (Wang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Running & Faris, 2024). 84 This limits our understanding of how effectorR gene interactions shape phenotypic 85 variation  among Pst pathotypes  and  influence  virulence  evolution  (Norman  et  al., 86 2023). To overcome these challenges, we adapted and optimised a high-sensitivity 87 protoplast-based screening system for functional testing of Pst effector candidates. 88 Building on the methodology described by Wilson et al. (2024), we incorporated the 89 self-replicating pWDV1 vector system to enhance transient expression of luciferase 90 reporters linked to defence activation. This optimisation significantly improved signal91 to-noise  ratio,  reduced  DNA  input  requirements,  and  increased  assay  throughput, 92 enabling scalable  and  reliable  detection  of Avr/R  interactions  in  wheat  protoplasts. 93 Thus,  this  platform  allows  detection  of Avr -triggered  immune  responses  in  wheat 94 cells, bypassing the limitations of whole-plant assays (Saur et al., 2019; González95 Grandío et al., 2021; Lubega et al., 2024). Using a fully phased genome assembly of 96 the  Australian Pst isolate  104E137A-,  where  both  haploid  nuclear  genomes  are 97

independently  resolved,  it  has  become  possible  to  identify  effector  genes  that  are hemizygous,  i.e.  present  in  only  one  of  the  two  haploid  nuclei.  These  single-copy effectors  are  of  particular  interest  because  hemizygosity  may  reflect  selective pressure to evade host recognition, representing an early stage in stepwise virulence evolution. By screening such candidates, we aimed to identify effectors with a high likelihood  of  avirulence  activity  that  may  be  differentially  recognised  by  specific wheat genotypes and potentially contribute to gene-for-gene resistance dynamics.

This  study characterises an evolutionary  conserved Pst effector  using  a  protoplast screening system and diverse wheat lines. In addition to confirming its recognition, we analysed the effector's sequence diversity, expression patterns during infection, and its impact on host defence responses. Our findings advance the understanding of  how  recognition  and  suppression  interplay  in  the Pst -wheat  pathosystem, providing insights into the evolution of pathogen virulence and strategies for durable resistance deployment.

MATERIAL AND METHODS

Candidate Pst Avr selection and gene synthesis

Avr candidates  were  previously  identified  by  analysing  a  time  course  of  long-read transcriptomic datasets of Pst 104E137A-, as detailed in Tam et al. (2025). Briefly, the transcripts  were  sampled  from  six  conditions  with  four  replicates  each,  including ungerminated urediniospores and infected wheat leaves at 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 days post-infection (dpi). All samples were subjected to direct Oxford Nanopore Technologies  (ONT)  cDNA  sequencing.  DESeq2  (Love  et  al.,  2014)  differential expression  analysis  was  conducted  on  transcript  read  abundance to identify secretome genes upregulated (log2 fold change ≥ 2) early during infection (4 and 6

Identification of AvrPstB48 paralogs

tblastn  v2.16.0+  was  used  to  search  for AvrPstB48 paralogs  using  its  translated protein sequence against the Pst 104E genome assembly (-evalue 1e-5). We applied a  low  identity  cut-off  of  0.4,  considering  effector  proteins  are  typically  sequencedivergent yet structurally conserved (Yu et al., 2023). The search results were used to  locate  the  annotated  gene  hits.  Multiple  protein  sequence  alignments  were performed using MAFFT v7.490 (Katoh & Standley, 2013) and visually inspected in Geneious to confirm overall similarities in physicochemical properties despite residue substitutions.

In silico structural modelling

The predicted tertiary structures of AvrPstB48 and its paralogs were generated using the AlphaFold Server v3.0 (DeepMind, UK) (Abramson et al., 2024). Models were generated based on the protein sequences with the signal peptides removed. The predicted  Template  Modelling  (pTM)  score  can  be  used  to  assess  global  model quality and  domain  orientation, where  scores ≥ 0.5 are generally considered acceptable  for  reliable  domain-level  predictions  (Jumper  et  al.,  2021).  The  initial scores for all structural  models were <0.5 (Supplementary Table S1). However, we observed  from  those  models  that  ~50  residues  at  the  N-terminus  of  each  protein were  unstructured. We also noted that the  sequences of the proteins  were  rich  in conserved cysteine residues. In other rust effectors, these have been shown to co148 ordinate Zn² + (Zhang et al., 2018; Outram et al., 2024). We therefore repeated the 149 predictions with the N-terminal truncated and Zn included. We observed an overall 150 improvement in the pTM scores for all models >0.5 when the N-terminal region was 151 removed,  and  this  was  generally  improved  when  Zn  was  added  to  the  prediction. 152 The  interface  prediction  score  for  Zn  binding  was  in  most  cases  >0.7,  indicating 153 acceptable interaction reliability (Abramson et al., 2024) (Supplementary Table S1). 154 The  resulting  models  were  visualised  and  analysed  using  ChimeraX  v1.8  (UCSF) 155 (Meng et al., 2023). Structural comparison of AvrPstB48 and its paralogs (P1, P2/P4, 156 and  P3)  was  performed  via  superimposition,  using AvrPstB48 as  the  reference 157 structure to identify conformational differences that may underlie functional 158 divergence. 159

Golden Gate assembly of expression constructs

The  hemizygous  effector  candidate  gene  ID Pst104E137_010232 ( AvrPstB48 ),  its paralogs Pst104E137_025766 ( AvrPstB48 _P1), Pst104E137_025767 ( AvrPstB48 _P2), Pst104E137_010231 ( AvrPstB48 _P3), and the mutants AvrPstB48 _D133G and AvrPstB48 _P3_G133D were digested with the BsaI restriction  enzyme,  creating  compatible  seamless  overhangs.  Simultaneously,  the digested  fragments  were  ligated  into  the pWDI expression  vector,  which  had  also been digested with BsaI .  The  reaction was cycled between 37°C for 3 minutes (to allow digestion) and 16°C for 4 minutes (for ligation) for 25 cycles, followed by a final incubation  at  50°C  for  5  minutes  and  80°C  for  5  minutes  to  inactivate  the BsaI enzyme.

Following the Golden Gate reaction, 5 µL of the reaction mixture was transformed into competent E. coli cells. The  cells  were  incubated  on  ice  for 30 minutes,  heatshocked  at  42°C  for  30  seconds,  and  then  returned  to  ice  for  5  minutes.  SOC medium (200 µL; containing 2% tryptone, 0.5% yeast extract, 10 mM NaCl, 2.5 mM KCl, 10 mM MgCl ₂ , 10 mM MgSO ₄ , and 20 mM glucose) was added, and the mixture was incubated at 37°C for 1 hour with shaking at 250 rpm.

Transformed E.  coli cells  were  plated  on  LB  agar  supplemented  with  35 μ g/mL chloramphenicol and incubated overnight at 37°C. Individual colonies were screened by colony PCR using gene-specific primers to confirm the presence of Avr candidate inserts (Supplementary Table S2). PCR-positive clones were cultured in LB broth for plasmid extraction using a standard miniprep protocol (Qiagen). Extracted plasmids were subjected to restriction enzyme digestion with insert-specific restriction sites to validate correct plasmid architecture. Clones  showing  the  expected  digestion patterns were then selected for full-length plasmid verification via ONT sequencing. Whole-plasmid sequencing  was performed using ONT MinION flow cells, and raw reads were analysed in Geneious Prime v2025.1.3 using Minimap alignment against the plasmid reference map to confirm correct insert orientation, sequence integrity, and absence of mutations (Li, 2018) (Supplementary Table S3).

Plant materials and growth conditions

Twelve  batches  of Pst -infected  plants  were  used  in  the  study. Two  trays  were prepared for the treatment group, and one tray was prepared for the control group. The control and treatment groups were grown in separate growth cabinets (18°C, 16 h  photoperiod,  300 μ mol/m²/s  light  intensity,  60%  relative  humidity  from  7  am  and 70% relative humidity from 11 pm). Ten grams of soluble fertiliser (HORTICO) was dissolved in 4.5 L of water, and 2.25 L was applied to each tray of plants. 100 mL of growth inhibitor (0.5% maleic hydrazide in Milli-Q water) was applied to each tray 8 days after sowing. Plants were watered every three days.

Protoplast isolation

Wheat protoplasts were isolated using the protocol described by Wilson et al. (2023, 2024),  with  minor  modifications.  Briefly,  wheat  cultivar  plants  were  grown  under controlled  conditions  in  a  growth  cabinet  for  7  to  9  days.  Protoplast  isolation  was achieved  by  making  shallow  incisions  on  the  leaf  epidermis  and  then  peeling  the epidermal layer to reveal the underlying mesophyll cells. The excised leaf segments were then incubated with the peeled surface in contact with a 0.6 M mannitol solution for  10 minutes. Subsequently, the segments were transferred to a well of a 12-well cell plate (2.3 cm) containing a filtered enzyme solution composed of MES buffer (pH 5.7,  20  mM),  mannitol  (0.8  M),  KCl  (10  mM),  cellulase  (Yakult  R-10)  1.5%  w/v, macerozyme (Yakult R-10) 0.75% w/v, CaCl2 (10 mM), and BSA 0.1% w/v. The plate was  wrapped  in  aluminium  foil  to  prevent  light  exposure  and  placed  on  an  orbital shaker  set  to  60  RPM  for  3  hours.  The  enzyme  solution  was  filtered,  and  the protoplasts were then collected by gentle centrifugation at 100 g for 3 minutes using a 14 mL round-bottom tube (17/100 mm). The enzyme solution was decanted, and the protoplast pellet was rinsed with W5 solution (MES buffer, pH 5.7, 2 mM; KCl, 5 222 mM; CaCl2, 125 mM; NaCl, 154 mM). Using a hemocytometer and light microscope, 223 a 10 µl aliquot of the concentrated protoplasts was taken for cell quantification and 224 quality assessment. The protoplasts were left at 4°C for 40 minutes to allow settling. 225 The  W5  solution  was  then  removed,  and  the  protoplasts  were  resuspended  to  a 226 concentration of 300,000-350,000 cells/mL using MMG solution (MES buffer, pH 5.7, 227 4 mM; mannitol, 0.8 mM; MgCl2, 15 mM). The protoplasts were used immediately for 228 subsequent transfection. 229

Protoplast transfection

Plasmid DNA (1 μ g/ μ L) was prepared using standard mini- and maxi-prep protocols (Promega  SV  Wizard,  Madison,  WI,  USA).  The  protoplast  transfection  protocol based on the method developed by Wilson et al. (2024) was adapted to a 96-well plate  format  to  increase  scalability  and  throughput.  A  wheat  codon-optimised luciferase  reporter  gene  (Luc)  was  cloned  into  the  pWDV1  self-replicating  vector under the control of the maize ubiquitin (UBI) promoter to enhance expression levels. Similarly,  all Avr candidates  and R genes  were  expressed  from  the  same  UBI promoter system in pWDV1 constructs.

For each transfection replicate, the following DNA amounts were used: 0.5 μ g of Avr plasmid, 10 μ g of R gene plasmid, and 5 μ g of the luciferase reporter plasmid. For Avr -only transfections, 1 μ g of Avr candidate plasmid and 5 μ g of luciferase plasmid were used. Plasmid DNA was pipetted into individual well of 96-well V-bottom plates (Costar). To each well, 50 μ L of freshly isolated wheat protoplasts and 50 μ L of PEG solution  (40%  w/v  PEG  4000,  0.2  M  mannitol,  100  mM  CaCl ₂ )  were  added  and gently  mixed by pipetting  up  and down five times. Monitoring luciferase expression

After the incubation, the protoplasts were prepared for luminescence measurement. The W5 supernatant was removed using a multichannel pipette, taking care not to disturb  the  protoplast  pellet.  50 μ L  of  1×  cell  lysis  buffer  (#E3971;  Promega)  was added, and the samples were gently mixed by pipetting 5 times, followed by a 15minute incubation. 50 μ L of lysate was collected and transferred to an opaque, flatbottom,  white,  solid  polystyrene  96-well  plate  (Corning  96-well  NBS  Microplate, #CLS3600).  The  total  luminescence  was  measured  using  a  plate  reader  (Tecan, Mannedorf, Switzerland; Infinite 200Pro, 1000 /i3 ms integration, 0 /i3 ms settle) without adding substrate to measure background signal. Then, 50 /i3μ l  Steady-Glo (#E2520; Promega)  Luciferin  substrate  was  added  to  each  well,  and  the  luminescence  was measured again. The plate reader was programmed  first to measure total luminescence without filters, using an integration time of 1000 ms and a settle time of 0 ms. Subsequently, luminescence was measured well-wise using green and red filters under the same acquisition settings.

Fresh spores of Pst isolate  104E137A- (Schwessinger et al.,  2018)  were collected from  infected  leaf  tissue.  Spores  were  suspended  in  3M  Novec  solution  and measured using a CellDrop Automated Cell Counter (DeNovix Inc.) to a concentration of approximately  5×10 5 spores/mL. Using a paintbrush, spores were inoculated on the upper surface of the first-emerged leaf of wheat plant 14 days after sowing. Infected plants were lightly sprayed with clean deionised water before being placed in an opaque, 25-L plastic container with lid (33 cm x 22.5 cm x 47 cm) and filled with water to 25% capacity. Containers with infected plants were wrapped with black plastic bags and incubated at 10°C in the dark to initiate Pst germination. After 48 hours, the infected plants were removed from the container and transferred back to the growth cabinet. Each infected leaf was assessed according to Dracatos et al. (2016) over 10 or more days post-infection (dpi).

Hyphae staining of infected wheat leaves

Staining was performed as described by Redkar et al. (2018). Freshly harvested leaf tissues were placed in 2 mL tubes containing 100% ethanol for three days or until majority of the chlorophyll had been removed. When the tissues were nearly devoid of  colour,  ethanol  was  replaced  with  10%  aqueous  potassium  hydroxide  and incubated  on  a  dry  heating  block  at  85°C  for  two  hours.  Cleared  tissues  were carefully  washed  10  times  in  1x  phosphate-buffered  saline  (PBS  Thermo  Fisher Scientific,  28237),  pH  7.4. Propidium iodide (PI; Sigma-Aldrich, P4170)  was mixed with  wheat  germ  agglutinin  conjugated  with  AlexaFluor488  (WGA-AF488;  Thermo Fisher Scientific, W11261) to create a PI-WGA AF488 staining solution (20 μ g/mL PI, 10 μ g/mL WGA-AF488, and 0.1% Tween 20 in 1× PBS, pH 7.4). After discarding the last  PBS  wash,  PI-WGA-AF488  staining  solution  was  added  to  the  2  mL tubes to

Stained tissues were  washed  twice  in  1x  PBS  to  remove  excess  staining  solution,  transferred  to fresh 1x PBS and stored at 4°C in the dark.

Callose staining of infected wheat leaves

Freshly harvested leaf tissues were placed in 2 mL tubes containing 1x PBS, pH 7.4 and vacuum infiltrated at 7 millibars until they sank to the bottom of the tube. Prior to staining,  leaf  tissues  were  hand-sectioned paradermally using a razor blade.  Eight pieces  of  sectioned  leaf  tissue  were  placed  in  each  well  of  the  96-well  plate  and washed  with  200 μ L  1x  PBS.  Tissues  were  stained  with  0.1%  aniline  blue  (AB; Sigma-Aldrich, 415049) in 1x PBS for 60 mins with vacuum infiltration and washed twice  in  1x  PBS.  WGA-AF488  staining  solution  (10 μ g/L  WGA-AF488  and  0.1% Tween 20 in 1× PBS, pH 7.4) was added to the samples and vacuum infiltrated at 7 millibars  for  5  times,  2  minutes  each,  with  2-minute  vacuum  release  in  between. Samples were washed twice in 1x PBS, transferred to fresh 1x PBS and stored at 4°C in the dark.

Confocal fluorescence microscopy

Stained leaf tissues were mounted onto a glass slide with 1x PBS and imaged under a TCS SP8 confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems) using 20x (PI-WGA-AF488) and 63x (AB-WGA-AF488) water-immersion objectives. WGA-AF488-stained fungal structures was visualised at 500-540 nm following excitation with an Argon laser at 488 nm. PI-stained cell wall was visualised at 580-630 nm following excitation with a DPSS laser at 561 nm. AB-stained callose was visualised at 430-500 nm following 321 UV excitation at 405 nm. To obtain a composite image with greater depth of field, Z322 stacking  was  performed  with  each  optical  section  captured  at  0.1 μ m  interval. 323 Images were processed (e.g., maximum projection, brightness and contrast) using 324 Leica Application Suite X software (LASX, Leica Microsystems). 325

Data collection and statistical analysis

Differences  in  normalised  luminescence  values  were  assessed  using  one-way analysis  of  variance  (ANOVA)  followed  by  Tukey's  Honestly  Significant  Difference (HSD) post hoc test (Wilson et al., 2024). The infectious hyphae cover area per leaf surface area was quantified using images obtained from PI-WGA-AF488-stained leaf tissues.  Meanwhile,  the  callose  signal  area  was  quantified  using  images  obtained from  AB-WGA-AF488-stained  leaf  tissues.  These  quantifications  were  performed using  ImageJ  software  (National  Institutes  of  Health)  on  a  Wacom  Cintiq  graphics tablet  (Wacom  Technology).  Statistical  analysis  of  data  obtained  from  confocal images was performed using Welch's t-test.

- RESULTS 338
- A conserved stripe rust candidate effector triggers defence signalling in wheat 339
- protoplasts 340

The  Avr  candidates  were  sourced  from  previous  work  on  the  nuclear  haplotype341 phased genome of isolate Pst 104E (Tam et al.,  2025). The candidate genes  were 342 identified based on the predicted presence of an N-terminal signal peptide, absence 343 of a transmembrane domain in the mature protein sequence, as well as 344

transcriptional upregulation during early wheat infection. From this set of candidate 345 effectors,  we  identified  and  functionally  uncharacterised AvrPstB48 as  an  effector 346 candidate.  The  name AvrPstB48 reflects  its  predicted  avirulence  function  'Avr'  in 347 Pst ,  with  'B'  representing  its  broad  recognition  pattern  across  diverse  wheat 348 cultivars,  and  '48'  corresponding  to  its  internal  candidate  ID  in  the  screening 349 pipeline. 350

Based  on  this  candidate  prioritisation  pipeline,  we  selected  a  panel  of  high351 confidence effector candidates for functional validation in wheat protoplasts. 352 Additional candidates, including Pst104E137_008104 (#47) and 353 Pst104E137_024713 (#49),  were  selected  using  the  same  criteria  and  served  as 354 comparisons for validation assays. 355

Using  a  luciferase-based  reporter  assay  under  the  control  of  the  D14  defence promoter  (Wilson  et  al.,  2024),  we  observed  a  robust  and  specific  increase  in ratiometric  luminescence  following  co-expression  of AvrPstB48 and  the  defence reporter,  indicating  a  recognition-dependent  defence  response  in  Avocet Yr5 and Avocet S (Figure 1). The  increase in ratiometric luminescence  observed  in AvrPstB48 was  consistent  with  the AvrSr50/Sr50 gene  pair,  which  served  as  a positive control to validate the assay performance (Supplementary Table S4) (Wilson et al., 2024). By contrast, other hemizygous candidates, #47 and #49, did not induce measurable reporter activity similar to negative control, AvrSr50 (Figure 1). Together, these results are consistent with AvrPstB48 encoding a recognised effector capable of activating host defence responses in specific wheat backgrounds (Supplementary Table S5).

AvrPstB48 recognition is conserved across multiple wheat cultivars

To  further  assess  defence  promoter  activation  triggered  by AvrPstB48 and  its paralogs,  we  tested  their  activities  in  additional  wheat  cv.  including  Avocet  S, Morocco, Triticum  spelta Yr5 ,  Chinese  166  and  Gabo,  using  three  previously validated  defence-associated  promoters  (D2,  D14,  and  D15)  (Wilson  et  al.,  2024). Additional screening of 16 wheat cultivars confirmed consistent activation in all Avocet near-isogenic lines, while eight cultivars showed no measurable response (Supplementary Table S5). Pathogen recognition slows infection and enhances early defence in susceptible hosts

To  explore  whether Avr recognition  might  influence  infection  dynamics  even  in  a susceptible  genotype,  we  compared  Avocet  S  and  Morocco,  both  of  which  are susceptible to Pst104E but with  contrasting AvrPstB48 responses. While Avocet S exhibits strong activation of a defence reporter in protoplast assays, Morocco shows no  such  activation,  making  these  genotypes  ideal  for  assessing  whether Avr -triggered  immunity  affects  pathogen  progression in  planta .  At  8  dpi,  neither  line exhibited macroscopic uredospore formation. By 10 dpi, both displayed visible rust symptoms,  however,  Morocco  developed  more  extensive  uredospore  coverage, suggesting  a  faster  rate  of  fungal  colonisation  (Figure  7A).  Confocal  microscopy supported these observations, revealing more abundant and earlier hyphal growth in Morocco compared to Avocet S at 8 dpi (Figure 7B). Although both lines were heavily colonised by 10 dpi, Avocet S retained localised areas with reduced fungal presence, consistent with a delayed pathogen development.

To  assess  host  immune  responses  underlying  these  differences,  we  examined callose deposition, a hallmark of plant defence. At 8 dpi, Avocet S exhibited greater callose accumulation than Morocco, particularly around infection sites (Figure 7C), and this was supported by quantitative analysis (Figure 7E). These findings indicate that  the  defence  response  in  Avocet  S  may  be  linked  to  its  ability  to  recognise AvrPstB48 ,  consistent  with  protoplast  assay  results  (Figure  1).  Conversely,  in Morocco, the absence of AvrPstB48 recognition correlates with more rapid colonisation and reduced immune activation (Figure 7D).

Discussion

Recent  advances  in  high-quality  genome  assemblies  of Pst (Wang  et  al.,  2024), coupled with advances  in effector prediction and  functional genomics, have significantly enhanced  our  capacity  to investigate host-pathogen  interactions. Additionally, by integrating comprehensive effector libraries and protoplast transformation  methods,  we  gained  deeper  insights  into  plant  immunity  dynamics (Arndell  et  al.,  2024;  Wilson  et  al.,  2024).  In  this  study,  we  characterised  an evolutionarily  conserved  avirulence  candidate  effector, AvrPstB48 ,  that triggers defence  responses  in  wheat.  Through  a  combination  of  protoplast-based  assays, structural modelling and plant phenotyping, we investigated how AvrPstB48 and its paralogs interact with wheat immune systems and influence resistance outcomes.

A central finding  of  this  work  is  the  genotype-dependent  recognition  of AvrPstB48 .

Strong defence activation was observed in wheat cultivars Avocet S, T. spelta Yr5 , and  Gabo,  while  others,  like  Morocco  and  Chinese  166,  showed  no  detectable response. This supports the hypothesis that AvrPstB48 recognition  is mediated  by specific R genes, likely  encoded by  NLR proteins. However,  we also acknowledge the possibility that the defence activation observed in protoplasts may be, at least in part,  a  consequence  of  overexpression  products  inherent  to  the  transient  assay system. Nonetheless, the genotype-specific nature of the responses, the absence of activation by unrelated candidates, and  the  correlation  with  delayed  disease phenotypes in  planta all  support  the  presence  of  biologically  relevant  recognition events. Recent studies have revealed that some stripe rust resistance genes exist as head-to-head NLR gene pairs, such as those derived from wild emmer wheat (Hu et al.,  2025).  Recognition  variability  highlights  the  co-evolutionary  dynamics  between Pst effectors  and  host  immune  receptors,  where  effectors  diversify  to  escape

596

597

recognition,  and  plants  evolve  new  surveillance  mechanisms  (Dodds  &  Rathjen, 584 2010; Asghar et al., 2024). 585 Our analysis of AvrPstB48 paralogs revealed functional redundancy  among 586 AvrPstB48 _P1 and AvrPstB48 _P2/P4. These paralogs retained the ability to activate 587 defence in cultivars that recognised AvrPstB48 , suggesting overlapping or 588 complementary roles. This redundancy may reflect an evolutionary strategy by Pst to 589 ensure virulence through multiple, functionally convergent effectors (Mukhtar et al., 590 2016;  Ibn-Salem  et  al.,  2017).  Such  strategies  have  been  documented  in  other 591 pathosystems, underscoring the importance of assessing effector families 592 collectively rather than in isolation (Mukhtar et al., 2011; Win et al., 2012). Moreover, 593 the extensive genetic diversity and nuclear reassortment reported across global Pst 594 populations highlight the dynamic evolutionary backdrop against which these effector 595 diversification and redundancy events occur (Hovmøller et al., 2025) In contrast, AvrPstB48 _P3 failed to trigger defence responses in any tested cultivar.

Structural  modelling  and  mutational  analyses  revealed  that  a  single  amino  acid substitution,  glycine  at  position  133  instead  of  aspartic  acid,  possibly  disrupts  its recognition.  This  substitution  probably  alters  the  electrostatic  surface  properties  of the  protein,  impairing  its  interaction  with  the  host  defence  receptor  (Tang  et  al., 2019). A  similar  effect  was  previously  observed  by  Manser  et  al.  (2021),  where  a mutation  from  aspartic  acid  to  glycine  in  the  Pm2a  resistance  protein  abolished recognition of AvrPm2. This is also true in the Pgt effector AvrSr50, where a single amino  acid  substitution  abolished  recognition  by  the  Sr50  receptor  (Chen  et  al., 2017). These observations demonstrate how minor sequence changes in effectors or R  genes  can  disrupt  immune  activation.  Substituting  aspartic  acid  for  glycine  in AvrPstB48 abolished  its  defence-triggering  ability,  indicating  that  residue  133  is

However,  introducing  the  reciprocal  G133D  substitution into AvrPstB48 _P3 did not restore activity, suggesting that other polymorphisms or conformational features interfere with recognition. This illustrates that recognition is not  determined  by  a  single  residue  but  instead  results  from  a  complex  structural context,  possibly  involving  epistatic  interactions  among  residues  that  influence effector  stability  or  surface  topology  (Kroj  et  al.,  2016;  Ortiz  et  al.,  2022).  These observations extend to previous findings where mutations in surface-exposed loops modulate Avr/R interactions  (Manser  et  al.,  2021;  Ortiz  et  al.,  2022).  In  our  study, residue 133 is located in a predicted loop region, supporting the notion that flexible, surface-accessible  regions  of  effectors  often  play  critical  roles  in  recognition.  The inability of the AvrPstB48 _P3_G133D mutant to restore defence activity implies that recognition is dependent on a combination of conserved sequence motifs and overall protein structure, rather than on single polymorphisms alone.

A significant insight from this study is the disconnect between defence activation in protoplasts and resistance phenotypes in whole plants.  Although AvrPstB48 consistently activated defence signalling in protoplast assays, cultivars like Avocet S remained  fully  susceptible  in  foliar  infection  assays.  This  discrepancy  underscores the complexity of immune responses in planta ,  where effectors may be recognised but  downstream  responses  are  suppressed  (Saur  et  al.,  2019;  Wu  &  Derevnina, 2023). We propose that AvrPstB48 is recognised in some wheat cultivars, triggering early immune responses, but this recognition is masked by other Pst effectors with suppressor functions. Comparative infection assay between Avocet S and Morocco support this hypothesis. Both cultivars are susceptible to Pst104E, yet only Avocet S recognised AvrPstB48 in protoplasts. In planta , Avocet S exhibited delayed disease progression, reduced mycelial growth, and increased callose deposition compared to

While  the  underlying  mechanisms  remain  to  be  fully  resolved,  the phenotypes observed in Avocet S are consistent with a transient defence response that  may  be  associated  with AvrPstB48 ,  followed  by  immune  suppression  that ultimately allows pathogen proliferation. Also, Avocet S is known to carry at least one functional resistance gene capable of recognising avirulent isolates of Pst (Nazari & Amil, 2013). The concept of effector-triggered susceptibility (ETS), wherein suppressor  effectors  negate  recognition-triggered  defences,  has  been  previously reported (Zambino et al., 2000; Zhang et al., 2023) and may also be present in the Pstwheat interaction.

This study  provides a comprehensive characterisation of AvrPstB48 ,  an  avirulence candidate  effector  in Pst ,  revealing  its  capacity  to  elicit  genotype-specific  immune responses  in  wheat.  Through  an  integrated  approach  combining  high-throughput protoplast assays, structural modelling, and in planta phenotyping, we demonstrate that AvrPstB48 recognition alone is insufficient to confer effective resistance. Instead, our  findings  support  a  model  in  which Avrtriggered  immune  responses  can  be actively suppressed, resulting in delayed or incomplete resistance.

The  functional  divergence  among AvrPstB48 paralogs  highlights  how  discrete structural  changes  can  abolish  recognition.  This  underscores  the  importance  of evaluating effector structure holistically, considering both conserved  motifs and the spatial  arrangement  of  surface-exposed  residues.  The  inability  of  cultivars  like Avocet S to mount effective resistance, despite recognising AvrPstB48, illustrates the limitations of reductionist systems and the need to interpret Avr/R interactions within the broader context of the pathogen effector collection.

- Together, these findings reinforce the complex nature of the plant  immune 659
- landscape,  shaped  by  a  dynamic  balance  of  recognition  and  suppression.  They 660
- emphasise the need for functional assays that account for multi-effector interactions 661
- and the role of suppressors in determining resistance outcomes. 662

figures.caption: Figure 1 pDefense D14 promoter activation triggered by AvrPstB48 in Avocet Yr5 and Avocet S  protoplasts .  Luciferase-based  reporter  assays  were  performed  using  wheat  protoplasts  from cultivars  Avocet Yr5 and  Avocet  S  to  assess  defence  signalling.  The  x-axis  shows  the  plasmid combinations tested, including candidate effectors Pst104E137_008104 (#47), Pst104E137_010232 ( AvrPstB48 ), and Pst104E137_024713 (#49), along with the positive control pair AvrSr50/Sr50 . The yaxis represents normalised luminescence values, measured as the ratio of luciferase activity driven by the  D14  promoter  to  the  internal  control  UBI-pRed. AvrPstB48 triggered  a  significant  increase  in reporter  activity,  indicating  activation  of  defence  responses.  Statistical  differences  were  analysed using  one-way ANOVA  followed  by  Tukey's  Honestly  Significant  Difference  (HSD)  test;  treatments sharing a common letter are not significantly different (p > 0.05).
figures.name: Figure 1
references: Using a luciferase-based reporter assay under the control of the D14 defence promoter (Wilson et al., 2024), we observed a robust and specific increase in ratiometric luminescence following co-expression of AvrPstB48 and the defence reporter, indicating a recognition-dependent defence response in Avocet Yr5 and Avocet S (Figure 1).
By contrast, other hemizygous candidates, #47 and #49, did not induce measurable reporter activity similar to negative control, AvrSr50 (Figure 1).

metadata.preprint_title: A conserved stripe rust effector elicits variable resistance responses in wheat

pub_metadata.preprint_title: A conserved stripe rust effector elicits variable resistance responses in wheat
pub_metadata.preprint_authors: Pereira, E. C.; Dagvadorj, B.; Tam, R.; Li, H.; Baig, D. I.; Moller, M.; Raphael, M.; Williams, S. J.; Periyannan, S.; Danila, F. R.; Rathjen, J. P.; Schwessinger, B.
pub_metadata.preprint_abstract: Plant resistance (R) and pathogen avirulence (Avr) gene interactions are central to pathogen recognition and disease resistance in crops. Functional characterisation of recognised Avr effectors of Puccinia striiformis f. sp. tritici (Pst) lags other key fungal pathogens of wheat. Here, we used a wheat protoplast-based screen to identify Avr/R interactions via the proxy of effector-induced defence responses in a set of diverse wheat cultivars. We identified an Avr candidate, termed AvrPstB48, that triggers defence responses in 16 out of 24 cultivars tested. AvrPstB48 is hemizygous, and the Pst genome carries four divergent paralogs within a gene cluster. Analysis of these paralogs revealed partial redundancy in their ability to activate wheat defences and enabled us to identify a single amino acid in AvrPstB48 that is necessary but not sufficient for defence activation. Notably, the activation of defence signalling by AvrPstB48 in protoplasts did not directly correlate with disease outcomes. Whole-plant infection assays revealed that some cultivars which exhibited strong defence activation in the protoplast assay are susceptible to the Pst isolate Pst104E137A- from which AvrPstB48 is derived. Comparison of infection dynamics of two wheat cultivars that differ in their AvrPstB48 recognition capacity revealed a delay in disease progression in the recognising cultivar Avocet S compared to the non-recognising cultivar Morocco. While correlative only, our observations, combined with other recent reports, support a  recognize-then-suppress model of plant-pathogen interaction where disease outcomes are driven not only by simple Avr/R interactions but also by pathogen effectors that suppress defence signalling downstream of effector recognition.
